name: Trees
coreIntuition: A tree is a connected graph with no cycles — which means there's exactly ONE path between any two nodes. This uniqueness property is what makes trees special: any property of the tree can be computed by breaking it into subtrees. Every tree problem is a recursion problem in disguise — the root's answer depends on its children's answers, which depend on their children's answers, all the way to the leaves.
mentalModel: Think of a tree as a hierarchy where every node is the 'boss' of its subtree. When the CEO (root) needs a report, each VP (child) collects reports from their departments and passes a summary upward. This bottom-up information flow IS tree recursion. Top-down is the reverse: the CEO sends instructions that cascade down. Most tree problems are either bottom-up (return values from leaves to root) or top-down (pass parameters from root to leaves).
whenToUse: The input is explicitly a tree (binary tree, BST, n-ary tree)
The problem has hierarchical structure (files, org chart, DOM)
You need to compute properties of subtrees
The problem involves paths in a tree (diameter, longest path, path sum)
You need to serialize, traverse, or reconstruct a tree

thinkingFramework.step: 1. Determine what you need to compute for each node
thinkingFramework.detail: What does each node need to 'know' about its subtree? Height? Size? Sum? Whether it's balanced? This becomes the return value of your recursive function.
Bottom-up: children return information to parent (height, size, validity). Top-down: parent passes information to children (depth, path sum so far, is-left-child). Some problems need both.
Express the node's answer in terms of its children's answers. Tree height: 1 + max(height(left), height(right)). Tree diameter: max(height(left) + height(right), diameter(left), diameter(right)).

thinkingFramework.step: 4. Handle base cases — null nodes and leaves
thinkingFramework.detail: What's the answer for a null node? For a leaf? These are your recursion base cases. Height of null = -1 (or 0 depending on convention). Height of leaf = 0.

Pre-order (root → left → right): good for serialization, copying. In-order (left → root → right): gives sorted order for BSTs. Post-order (left → right → root): good for deletion, bottom-up computation.

commonTraps.trap: Computing the same thing multiple times (e.g., height inside diameter)
commonTraps.howToAvoid: Return multiple values from your recursive function. Instead of computing height separately, return it alongside your main answer. This turns O(n²) into O(n).
Define what your function returns for null input FIRST. This is your base case. Test: does your solution work on a single-node tree? On an empty tree?

commonTraps.trap: Confusing BST properties with general tree properties
commonTraps.howToAvoid: BST: left < root < right for ALL nodes in subtrees (not just immediate children). Validate BST by passing min/max bounds down, not by checking just parent-child.

interviewVariantStrategy: Tree problems come in layers of difficulty: (1) **Basic traversal**: pre/in/post/level order — know all four cold. (2) **Property computation**: height, diameter, balance, symmetry — these are recursion exercises. (3) **Path problems**: path sum, longest path, LCA — require tracking information along paths. (4) **Construction**: build tree from traversals, serialize/deserialize — require understanding how traversal orders uniquely identify trees. (5) **BST-specific**: validate BST, kth smallest, successor/predecessor — exploit the sorted property. The interview twist: many tree problems are really about computing two things simultaneously (e.g., diameter needs both height AND max-path-through-each-node).

resources.title: NeetCode — Binary Tree Problems
resources.why: Structured progression through tree problems from basic traversal to advanced tree DP

resources.title: Abdul Bari — Tree Data Structure
resources.why: Clear foundational explanation of tree concepts, traversals, and properties

resources.title: VisuAlgo — Binary Search Tree
resources.why: Interactive BST visualization — insert, delete, search, and see rotations in real time

resources.title: Striver — Binary Tree Series
resources.why: Comprehensive tree problem series covering all major interview patterns with clear explanations
